title: Is AI Making You Dumber? It Depends on When You Use It
summary:
Next time you're about to ask an AI chatbot for help, you might want to pause. A small but intriguing study presented at the 2026 CHI conference in Barcelona suggests that when you use AI matters a lot for your critical thinking.

Computer scientist Mina Lee from the University of Chicago and her team assigned 393 people to eight different groups. Participants played the role of a city council member deciding on a water contamination proposal. They had either 30 minutes (sufficient time) or 10 minutes (insufficient time), and could use OpenAI's GPT-4o chatbot early, continuously, late, or not at all.

The results were clear: the group with sufficient time and late chatbot access scored highest on essay quality, incorporating more perspectives and valid arguments. However, the group that remembered the most information from the documents was the one with sufficient time and no chatbot access at all. Under time pressure, early chatbot access boosted essay scores.

Barbara Oakley, an education expert at Oakland University, explains that this aligns with two types of learning: slow, effortful reasoning and fast, automatic thinking. Participants who had time to think first engaged in slower learning, then used AI to enhance their work. Those under pressure relied on fast thinking, which AI can support but at the cost of deeper engagement.

Lee warns that using AI early under time pressure risks adopting the AI's framing without fully processing the information. "You have to at least be aware of what you're signing up for," she says. The key takeaway? Develop AI literacy and know your own thinking patterns to decide when to use chatbots wisely.
why_it_matters: For a 12-year-old using AI to help with homework, this research shows that letting yourself think about a problem first before asking a chatbot can actually make you smarter, while instantly copying an AI's answer might leave you less able to solve the next problem on your own—so the way you use AI today shapes your future thinking skills.

keywords.explanation: Interesting in a way that makes you want to know more, often because something is unusual or mysterious.
Having enough of something, like time or resources, to complete a task well.
Not having enough of something, like time or money, which can lead to rushed decisions.
When something becomes dirty or harmful by mixing with unwanted substances, like polluted water.
Requiring a lot of mental energy and focus, like solving a hard puzzle.
The ability to understand and use something well, like knowing how to read and use AI tools wisely.

questions.question: What was the main finding of the study about AI use and critical thinking?
options: A) Using AI early always improves essay scores.
B) Late AI access with enough time produced the best essay quality.
C) No AI access led to the lowest essay scores overall.
D) Time pressure had no effect on how people used AI.
questions.correct_answer: B) Late AI access with enough time produced the best essay quality.

questions.question: Which group remembered the most information from the documents?
options: A) The group with early AI access under time pressure.
B) The group that used AI continuously with sufficient time.
C) The group with sufficient time and no chatbot access at all.
D) The group that used AI late but had insufficient time.
questions.correct_answer: C) The group with sufficient time and no chatbot access at all.

questions.question: According to Barbara Oakley, what are the two types of learning mentioned?
options: A) Digital and analog learning.
B) Slow, effortful reasoning and fast, automatic thinking.
C) Group learning and independent learning.
D) Abstract reasoning and concrete thinking.
questions.correct_answer: B) Slow, effortful reasoning and fast, automatic thinking.

questions.question: What did Mina Lee warn about using AI early under time pressure?
options: A) It makes people more creative in their arguments.
B) It risks adopting the AI's framing without fully processing the information.
C) It leads to better long-term memory of facts.
D) It helps people question the AI's answers more carefully.
questions.correct_answer: B) It risks adopting the AI's framing without fully processing the information.

questions.question: Which AI tool was used in the study?
options: A) OpenAI's DALL-E
B) Google's Bard
C) OpenAI's GPT-4o
D) Microsoft's Copilot

questions.question: What does Lee suggest as the key takeaway from the study?
options: A) Never use AI for schoolwork.
B) Develop AI literacy and understand your own thinking patterns.
C) Always rely on AI to save time.
D) Only use AI after finishing all your work.
questions.correct_answer: B) Develop AI literacy and understand your own thinking patterns.

background_read: The 2026 CHI conference in Barcelona is the premier international gathering for human-computer interaction researchers, where they share how technology affects people's lives.

Barbara Oakley, a professor of engineering at Oakland University and author of 'A Mind for Numbers,' popularized the idea that our brain switches between focused and diffuse modes of thinking, which parallels the slow and fast reasoning she describes here.

The concept of 'cognitive offloading'—letting tools store or remember information for us—has been studied for years; this experiment extends that idea to AI chatbots, showing that the timing of offloading can either strengthen or weaken deep thinking.

Dual-process theory in psychology, first detailed by Daniel Kahneman, distinguishes between two thinking systems: System 1 (fast, automatic) and System 2 (slow, effortful). This study's results align with that model, showing that using AI early may bypass the deeper System 2 engagement needed for true learning.

While the study used OpenAI's GPT-4o, a large language model chatbot, the lessons apply broadly to any AI assistant that can instantly provide answers, suggesting that schools might need to teach not just 'AI literacy' but also when to pause before prompting.
Article_Structure: HOOK: Is AI making you dumber? It depends on when you use it — pause before asking.
├─ WHO: 393 people, Mina Lee from University of Chicago
├─ WHAT: City council water contamination decision with 30 min or 10 min, using GPT-4o early, continuously, late, or never
├─ SUFFICIENT TIME + LATE AI: highest essay quality
├─ SUFFICIENT TIME + NO AI: best memory of documents
└─ TIME PRESSURE + EARLY AI: improved essay scores but shallow processing
└─ Barbara Oakley: slow, effortful reasoning vs. fast, automatic thinking
└─ Mina Lee: early AI under pressure risks adopting AI's framing without understanding
TAKEAWAY: develop AI literacy and know your own thinking patterns

perspectives.perspective: Positive: strategic AI use enhances performance
perspectives.description: When people think deeply about a problem first and then use AI to check or expand their ideas, the final work is stronger. This shows AI can be a powerful tutor that helps sharpen arguments rather than replacing thinking.

perspectives.perspective: Negative: early or rushed reliance weakens learning
perspectives.description: Under time pressure, the temptation to grab the AI's ready-made frame can short-circuit original analysis. Over time, this habit might erode a person's ability to reason independently or remember key information, making them more dependent.

perspectives.perspective: Neutral: the pattern fits dual-process theory
perspectives.description: The study's findings are a real-world demonstration of two thinking systems: the quick, intuitive system that AI easily triggers, and the slower, more deliberate system that builds lasting understanding. Neither system is bad; the context decides which one is appropriate.

perspectives.perspective: Forward-looking: the need for AI literacy education
perspectives.description: Schools and families will likely need to teach not just how to use AI tools but when to pause and think independently. Future research could help create personalized guidelines that match a person's thinking style, ensuring AI becomes a booster for the brain rather than a crutch.
